phrase_or_pattern: The rise at the end of Czech questions
origin: Klára and Markéta talking — questions went up at the end, statements went down, the musicality of Czech entering her ears before she knew it was different from the noise outside

phrase_or_pattern: The soft 'šššš' hush
origin: Klára's anxious hush — the Czech way, softer than English 'shhh', drawn out, used when Anna cried but also when Klára was trying to calm herself, Anna would always associate this sound with being worried-about rather than soothed

phrase_or_pattern: The low rumble of English singing
origin: Marcus in the bathroom — 'Yellow Submarine,' 'Hey Jude,' the Beatles because that's what his dad played, his voice going low and silly and different from the high Czech sounds, she didn't know it was English yet, just that it came from a different place

phrase_or_pattern: 'More' — first word, used for everything, said with reaching hands
origin: From Dad giving her McVitie's Digestives and her reaching and him saying 'do you want more?' until the word became hers, became the key to everything

phrase_or_pattern: Answering Czech with English, always English, the wrong language on purpose or not on purpose
origin: From Peppa Pig, from Mr Tumble, from the park, from the nursery kids, from Dad's singing, from the whole English world pushing in through every crack in the flat like the cold air that made the radiator knock

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'man inside' about the radiator — her own construction, her own logic
origin: From the knocking that sounded like fists, from not understanding pipes or boilers or central heating, from the way her brain made sense of sounds that had no visible source — if it knocks like hands then hands must be making it

phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' — used it like English, thought it was just what you said when you bumped into things
origin: Klára said it every single day when she hit the fridge. Every. Single. Day. Anna thought it was the English word for ouch or for the fridge or for being tired. She didn't know it was Czech for 'for God's sake' until Markéta Dvořáková laughed when Anna said it at her flat and explained. She was six. She felt stupid.

phrase_or_pattern: Peppa Pig phrases as incantations — 'muddy puddles,' 'dinosaur,' 'I'm Peppa Pig' — used them like spells, like saying the words would make the world behave
origin: The DVD. The endless DVD. The episodes watched until the disc physically degraded. She would walk around the flat narrating like the show — 'I'm Anna Pig, this is my little brother George' — even though she didn't have a brother, even though her name wasn't Pig, even though none of it matched but the rhythm of it was the rhythm of safety.

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'more' with reaching hands — still doing this at two, almost three, the word becoming a demand not a request
origin: The first word. The most powerful word. More stickers. More Peppa. More biscuits. More stones. More Dad. The word was a key that opened doors and she used it constantly, shamelessly, with her whole body reaching toward the thing she wanted.

phrase_or_pattern: Private sounds — not Czech, not English, just mouth-noises that meant something only to her
origin: The fish tank hours. The not-playing. The space between the Czech that lived in the flat and the English that lived everywhere else. Her brain, left with no input it wanted, started making output that belonged to neither world. She whispered to herself in sounds that had no translation, told herself stories in a language that didn't exist, and it was the first thing that was entirely hers.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Bude to dobré' — understood it as a lie before she understood it as words
origin: Klára said it every morning at drop-off. 'Bude to dobré, miláčku.' It will be good, darling. But her voice went high when she said it, and her hands were tense, and nothing about it was good. Anna learned the phrase as the sound Mum made when she was lying, and she believed it was a lie for years.

phrase_or_pattern: Peppa Pig phrases used as armor — 'I'm Peppa Pig' said to the nursery workers who asked her name
origin: She wouldn't say her name. She wouldn't say anything real. But she'd say 'I'm Peppa Pig' like a spell, like putting on a costume, like being someone else was the only way to be someone at all. The nursery workers wrote it down in her file. Klára was embarrassed. Anna was safe behind the pig.

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'more' with her whole body — the word plus reaching hands plus the lean toward the thing she wanted
origin: Still her most powerful word. Still worked on the swing. Still worked on biscuits. Still worked on Dad's attention. She hadn't learned yet that 'more' would stop working on some things, that you could say it and reach for it and the thing would still get smaller.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' — still thought it was English, still used it when she bumped into things, now also used it as a whisper when she heard the kitchen voices go sharp
origin: Mum's word, the fridge word, the ouch word — now it was also the I-heard-something-wrong word, a tiny spell she said to herself in bed when the tap was running and the voices had stopped and she needed something to make the quiet feel less like holding your breath

phrase_or_pattern: Peppa Pig phrases as comfort scripts — 'I'm Peppa Pig, this is my little brother George' even though she didn't have a brother
origin: The DVD. The endless DVD. The episodes she could recite. When the kitchen voices got loud she'd whisper Peppa lines into her pillow because the rhythm of them was the rhythm of safe, of predictable, of a story that always ended the same way with everyone laughing

phrase_or_pattern: Singing 'the Vikings came across the sea' under her breath during quiet times
origin: Horrible Histories, CBBC, every day at 3:45. The song became a reflex, something her mouth did when her brain was elsewhere, like the 'proboha' that Mum said at the fridge.

phrase_or_pattern: Answering Mum in English without thinking, then watching her face tighten
origin: The school voice got louder than the home voice. English was the language that worked — it got praise, it got results, it got you moved up in reading. Czech was becoming the language that didn't work, that marked you as different, that made Mum's face do the thing. She didn't choose to stop. It just happened. Like breathing.

phrase_or_pattern: Moshi Monsters vocabulary — 'Rox,' 'Moshlings,' 'Katsuma' — used like they were real things
origin: The membership. The hours on the Dell. The obsession that was the same shape as every obsession before it — the sticker books, the stones, the Peppa episodes. Collect, complete, empty, repeat.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Alright, Munch?' — parroting Dad's greeting back at him
origin: Dad's voice, low, warm, the rumble. She started saying it because he said it. Then she kept saying it because it made him smile. Then she kept saying it after the smiles got smaller.

phrase_or_pattern: Counting Czech words during arguments — 'proboha, ne, prosím, počkej' — tallying them on her fingers like a secret score
origin: The radiator pipe. The night arguments. The need to understand what was happening through the only method available — collecting the fragments she could name, hoping they'd form a picture. A survival mechanism disguised as a game.

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'fine' with the same tight mouth Mum used — mimicking the lie without knowing she was mimicking it
origin: Klára's morning voice after a night of crying. The 'everything's fine, Anna' that Anna started using at school when teachers asked how she was. She didn't know she'd learned it from Mum. She thought it was just what you said.

phrase_or_pattern: 'The man inside' — expanded now to mean the thing that carries secrets, not just the thing that knocks
origin: Her own construction. The radiator pipe had a man inside and the man knocked and the man also whispered and the man knew things. The metaphor grew because she needed it to grow — 'angry man' didn't cover what the pipe was doing now. 'Spy' was closer but she didn't use that word yet. 'Man inside' was enough.

phrase_or_pattern: Answering 'I don't know' when she meant 'I don't want to talk about it'
origin: A defensive structure built from months of hearing things she couldn't process. 'I don't know' was safer than 'I heard you through the pipe' or 'I saw Mum crying' or 'Why does Dad come home late?' — 'I don't know' was the wall she built around the rooms inside rooms.

phrase_or_pattern: The backwards language — 'nna' for Anna, 'arale' for Elara, 'emoh' for home
origin: Elara Chung's accidental 'gip' instead of 'pig' in the sitting room of her flat, the game that became a country, the language that kept the flat's secrets out because the secrets couldn't be translated backwards

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'it's fine' about things that are not fine, flat voice, no eye contact
origin: The spare room. The snoring excuse. The taken-back comments. She learned to close her own doors this way — not physical doors, but the doors to her face, her voice, the place where worry lived. 'It's fine' was the English version of Mum's 'proboha,' something you said to fill the space where honesty should go.

phrase_or_pattern: Dropping Czech words into English sentences without noticing — 'prosím' instead of 'please,' 'ano' instead of 'yes'
origin: The flat was more Czech now that Dad was in the spare room. Mum's relief at not having to perform English in the evening. The Czech returned when Dad left the room and Anna absorbed it like the wallpaper absorbed damp, without choosing to, without knowing it was happening.

phrase_or_pattern: Calling Dad's room 'the spare room' instead of 'Dad's room'
origin: Because it was the spare room first. Because calling it 'Dad's room' would make it permanent and she wasn't ready for permanent. Because 'the spare room' implied he could move back, that the slippers could return to the front door, that the migration might reverse.

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'fine' with a shrug when adults ask how she's doing
origin: The first week after Dad left, every teacher, every dinner lady, every parent at the gate asked 'how are you doing, love' with the soft face, and she learned that 'fine' was the word that made the soft face go away, that ended the conversation, that let her stop performing grief for people who wanted to witness it

phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' said more often, picked up from Mum's increase in saying it
origin: Mum said 'proboha' at everything now — burning the pasta, dropping a spoon, the news on TV, Anna's school bag in the wrong place — and Anna said it too, matching her, and she didn't think about whether it was Czech or English, it was just what you said when the world was too much

phrase_or_pattern: Answering 'I don't know' to questions she knew the answer to
origin: Mum asked 'why are you crying' at 3am and the answer was 'because Dad left and because you said he chose freedom and because I think it's my fault and because I wanted too much' but that was too many words and some of them were Czech and some were English and all of them were true so 'I don't know' was easier, 'I don't know' was the lid on the container

phrase_or_pattern: Calling it 'Dad's flat' instead of 'Dad's house' — flat, not home, never home
origin: He told her the address — a flat in Croydon, she didn't know Croydon, she knew Tooting and the flat above Ladbrokes — and she filed it as 'Dad's flat' in her head, a place he existed, not a place she belonged, the word 'home' was already taken and already broken

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'fine' and 'nothing' and 'yeah' as complete sentences
origin: Sunday Nando's. The questions Dad asked that she stopped really answering because the answers didn't land anywhere, they just fell into the space between them and dissolved. 'Fine' was a wall. 'Nothing' was a door she closed. 'Yeah' was whatever he needed to hear so he'd stop trying.

phrase_or_pattern: The name 'Siân' said internally with a flinch, like a word that bites
origin: Mum's mouth. The way Klára said it — 'Siân' — with the sharpness on the S and the flatness on the â, like the word itself was the problem. Anna absorbed the flinch. Now the name lives in her body as a small electric shock, even when she just thinks it.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' said with Mum's exact cadence, same situations, same tiredness
origin: The fridge bump. The dropped keys. The shopping bag splitting. Anna said it at school once — the dinner lady gave her a weird look — and realised she'd been speaking Czech without noticing, been channelling Mum without choosing to, been becoming the thing she watched.

phrase_or_pattern: Mouthing Horrible Histories lyrics instead of singing them
origin: The flat was too quiet for singing. Singing filled the silence and then the silence came back worse. Mouthing was safe. Mouthing was private. Mouthing was having the song without giving it to the room, because the room might take it and make it mean something else.

phrase_or_pattern: The cracked-screen vocabulary — 'whatever,' 'fine,' 'I don't care' — short words that close doors
origin: The throw. After the phone hit the wall she started using language like she used the crack — as evidence, as armor. Short sentences when she was uncomfortable, which was always. 'Whatever' as a shield. 'Fine' as a lie. 'I don't care' as the biggest lie. She learned that you can say less and feel more, that silence can be a weapon, that English is good for walls because it doesn't invite follow-up questions the way Czech does.

phrase_or_pattern: Refusing Czech — not speaking a single Czech word during the drive, during the ferry, during the first days, like English was the last piece of England she could keep
origin: The move. The kitchen table. The knowledge that Czech was the language of the place she was going and English was the language of the place she was leaving, and every Czech word she said was a step away from the flat above Ladbrokes and toward whatever came next. She held English like a rope. She didn't speak Czech for the entire drive, the entire ferry, the first week in Benešov, and the silence was deliberate, was political, was the only vote she had.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' — but weaponised now, said at Mum, not with Mum
origin: The fridge bump was accidental. The new 'proboha' was aimed. When Mum said 'it'll be okay, Anna,' Anna said 'proboha' like Mum said it — sharp, tired, for God's sake — and she watched Klára flinch and the flinching was the point. She took Mum's word and used it against her. The word had always been about the fridge and the bumping and the not-learning, but now it was about the move, the boxes, the cold tea, and every 'proboha' was a small revenge that didn't make her feel better but she did it anyway.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Potřebuju na záchod' — said carefully, practiced in her head for hours before saying, and still got it wrong
origin: Klára drilled this phrase before school started, sitting at the kitchen table in London, making Anna repeat it, 'potřebuju na záchod, potřebuju na záchod,' and Anna said it right thirty times at home and wrong once at school and the once was the one that lived forever.

phrase_or_pattern: Pointing instead of speaking — at the toilet, at food in the canteen, at the page when called on in class
origin: Self-invented survival strategy. The toilet incident proved that Czech was a minefield and her mouth was a liability. Pointing was universal. Pointing couldn't be wrong. Pointing couldn't be laughed at. She became fluent in gesture.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Fine' — the only word she said on FaceTime with Dad, said flat, said small, said like a door closing
origin: Not from anywhere. Just the only safe answer. Fine wasn't good, which was a lie, and fine wasn't bad, which would make him worry, and fine wasn't true, which was the point — the truth was too big for a cracked screen, too complicated for eleven minutes, too heavy for a Sunday evening.

phrase_or_pattern: The word 'angličanka' — said by Tereza, repeated by others, absorbed like a bruise
origin: Tereza Bártová, second day of school, whispered but not really, the way kids whisper when they want to be heard. The English girl. The foreign girl. The wrong girl. Anna didn't know the word was coming but she felt it arrive, the way you feel a stone thrown at your chest — the impact before the understanding.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Fakt' instead of 'opravdu' — the casual Czech that marks you as someone who actually speaks the language
origin: Kovy's YouTube videos. He said 'fakt' every third sentence and she started saying it before she knew what it replaced. When she said 'fakt?' to Matěj on Discord and he said 'jo, fakt' back, she felt something click — not just understanding but belonging.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Seš' instead of 'jsi' — the shortened spoken form no textbook teaches
origin: Matěj on Discord. He said 'seš vtipná' when she made a joke about his tower and she heard it as one word, not two. Had to ask him to repeat it twice before she understood it was 'jsi' in disguise. Now she says it without thinking. That's the point — the Czech that comes without thinking is the Czech that's becoming hers.

phrase_or_pattern: The Minecraft-Czech hybrid — 'postav to tam' and 'sem ne' and 'je to hotový?' — stripped-down purpose-driven Czech
origin: Hours on Discord. The Czech of Minecraft is different from school Czech — shorter, faster, full of 'tady' and 'tam.' The first complete Czech sentence she said without hesitation was 'postavme most' — let's build a bridge — and even at ten she felt the weight of that metaphor.

phrase_or_pattern: Dropping Czech words into English without noticing — 'that's fakt stupid' and 'she's so blbá'
origin: The two languages leaking into each other. She doesn't notice until Matěj points it out, laughing, saying 'you just said half-Czech.' The hybrid is its own language, neither English nor Czech, and it's the one that feels most like her even though it's not supposed to exist.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Dobře, dobře' — said it constantly, to herself, about herself, like a tic or a reassurance
origin: From Děda František at the chess table. His slow nod when she made a good move, the two words pressed together like they were one word, dobře-dobře, an approval that didn't make a big deal of itself. She started saying it in her head when she finished a run, when she understood a sentence in Czech, when she did anything right. It replaced 'more' as the word that meant good, that meant enough, that meant you did it.

phrase_or_pattern: Chess vocabulary in Czech — jezdec, střelec, věž, dáma, šach, mat
origin: From Děda, from the games, from the way he'd tap each piece and say its name when they first started playing, like he was introducing her to a room full of people. Jezdec was the knight because it rode, střelec was the bishop because it shot, and the names made more sense than the English ones, the pieces became characters with jobs instead of abstract shapes, and she never confused them after that.

phrase_or_pattern: Czech internet slang — 'konec', 'gg', 'trapas', words that lived on Discord and in YouTube comments
origin: From Matěj, from the Discord calls, from the Minecraft server where language was faster and looser than in classrooms. 'Trapas' became her favorite because it meant embarrassment and it sounded like what it was, like something you wanted to erase, and she used it constantly, describing every mistake she made as 'total trapas' in a way that made Matěj laugh.

phrase_or_pattern: Answering Mum's Czech with silence instead of English — the new refusal
origin: From the lockdown, from the suffocation, from the discovery that leaving the room was a sentence and silence was a language and both of them said 'you're too close' more clearly than words ever could. She stopped fighting in English. She just stopped engaging. The silence was louder than the wrong language had ever been.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Tvoje tah' — your turn — said it to herself when she was stuck, when she needed to move, when the waiting felt endless
origin: From Děda starting every game, his finger pointing at her side of the board, the phrase meaning it was her move, her choice, her responsibility. It became an internal prompt — tvoje tah, Anna, move, do something, you can't just sit here. She said it to herself before runs, before difficult homework, before FaceTime calls with Dad she didn't want to make.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Tvoje tah' — said to herself when she needs to make a decision, his voice in her head, the closest thing to guidance she has now
origin: Děda František's chess voice. He said it every time it was her turn — on the board, in the game, in life, though she didn't know that last part until he was gone and she started hearing it when she needed to hear it most.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Ne tak napjatě' — not so tense — said to her own shoulders when she catches herself clenching, a correction, a kindness from the grave
origin: Děda's coaching voice. He said it when she gripped the pieces too hard, when she leaned too far forward, when she wanted the game to be over faster than it could be. She didn't know it was a life lesson. She knows now.

phrase_or_pattern: Dropping Czech into her internal monologue without noticing — 'dobře' for okay, 'proboha' for everything, 'ani ne' for dismissal
origin: The immersion finally taking. After a year and a half in Benešov the Czech is becoming the first language instead of the second and she doesn't know when that happened. Mum would be relieved. She doesn't want Mum to be relieved. So she doesn't tell anyone that she dreams in Czech now. That she thinks in Czech. That English is becoming the foreign language and this terrifies her more than the grief does.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Sorry for your loss' — said in English with a flat voice, a mockery, a thing she repeats to herself to make it mean nothing
origin: The girls at school who learned the English phrase specially, who thought saying it in her other language would be kind, who made it worse by trying. She repeats it now like a spell in reverse, a ward against pity, a reminder that most kindness is just performance.

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'nevím' instead of longer answers — I don't know, shortened, made into a shield
origin: The new school. The back row. The survival strategy of answering every question with the shortest possible Czech. 'Nevím' became her default — not because she didn't know but because knowing out loud was a risk she wasn't willing to take.

phrase_or_pattern: Using 'tvoje tah' — your turn — in her head when she needs to make a decision
origin: Děda. The chess games. The patient voice. She hears it when she needs to move, to choose, to act. Not his voice exactly — the memory of his voice, which is different, which is hers now, which is the thing he left behind.

phrase_or_pattern: Dropping the formal 'vy' with Mrs. Navrátilová in her head — still saying it out loud, but thinking 'ty' — the intimacy of informal Czech that she hasn't earned yet
origin: The smile. The recognition. The way Mrs. Navrátilová looked at her and saw a student instead of a problem. Anna started thinking of her in the informal in January, maybe February, but she'd never say it out loud because the formal is respect and the informal is trust and trust takes longer than a smile.

phrase_or_pattern: Funeral Czech — 'upřímnou soustrast' and 'kondolence' learned by repetition, parroted without feeling
origin: The house full of relatives. Everyone said 'upřímnou soustrast' to Babička, to Mum, to her, and she said it back without knowing what the weight of the words was supposed to feel like. The phrase became empty through repetition. Like 'proboha.' Like all the Czech words she said because they were expected, not because she meant them.

phrase_or_pattern: Calling the canteen 'jidelna' with a flat voice, no article, the Czech way — not 'the canteen,' just 'jidelna,' a place stripped of comfort
origin: The table near the bins. The sour smell. The daily routine. 'Jidelna' became a single hard word in her mouth, not a place you go to eat, a place you go to survive.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Ta kráska' — said with bitter irony to herself, never aloud, a curse disguised as a compliment
origin: Jana at the barbecue, the hand on the shoulder, the smile that was supposed to be kind but felt like a brand. Anna took the phrase and made it a weapon she points at herself when she catches her reflection and feels the small warm feeling under the revulsion. Ta kráska. The beauty. The cage.

phrase_or_pattern: Mixed Czech-English with Tereza — 'that's so trap' next to 'to je hrozný,' switching mid-sentence without noticing
origin: The café on Náměstí. The back corner. Hours of scrolling and talking and the two languages bleeding into each other because Tereza watched English TikTok and Anna thought in Czech now, mostly, and the intersection was a country only they lived in. 'Trap' from English slang meaning embarrassing — Tereza used it wrong and Anna didn't correct her because the wrongness was theirs.

phrase_or_pattern: Calling running 'the off switch' — never to anyone, just in her head
origin: The first time her brain went quiet. The rhythm drowning the noise. She didn't have words for what running did so she made some up. The off switch. Like she was a machine that could be silenced. Like the radiator could stop knocking if she just ran fast enough.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Dobrý' instead of 'good' or 'dobře' — the casual Czech approval that doesn't require the full word
origin: Karolína Dvořáková after the time trial. 'Dobrý, Liebigová.' Not 'dobře' — that was what Děda said, that was careful, that was chess. 'Dobrý' was faster, looser, earned on a track instead of given across a board. Anna started using it for things that mattered — a good run, a good time, a good silence. Not for everything. Only for things that were actually good.

phrase_or_pattern: Mixed Czech-English with Tereza — 'that's so cringe, ale vážně, like proper cringe, šílený'
origin: Tereza Bártová watches English TikTok, Anna grew up with English, the combination became their private language, nobody else at school can follow it and that's the point, it's a border they control, a country with two citizens

phrase_or_pattern: 'Good for you' — said flat, said Czech, said wrong
origin: What she said when Tereza told her Ondřej had asked her out. She meant 'thank you for saying no' and she meant 'I hate this' and she meant 'I'm dying' and she said 'good for you' because the other things were too honest and honesty felt like bleeding in public. The phrase has calcified. She says it now when she means the opposite. Tereza knows. That's why it works.

phrase_or_pattern: Running terminology used as emotional shorthand — 'splits,' 'personal best,' 'lactic acid'
origin: From Coach Novotný, from Karolína Dvořáková, from the language of the track. When she can't describe how she feels about Ondřej she says 'bad split.' When something goes right she says 'personal best.' The metaphor is unconscious. She doesn't know she's doing it.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' — still using it, still Czech, still the word that comes out when she bumps into things or drops something or sees something that makes her stomach drop
origin: Klára. The fridge. The word that lives in her body before it lives in her mind. She said it once at school and someone laughed and she felt the old shame from when Markéta Dvořáková explained what it meant, but she can't stop saying it, it's not a choice, it's a reflex, it's the sound her mouth makes when the world goes wrong.

phrase_or_pattern: Calling Pavel 'on' — the Czech formal 'he' — instead of by name
origin: Started in January when he began staying over. She refused to say his name in the house because naming him would make him real, would make him permanent. 'On' was distance. 'On' was a wall built out of a pronoun. Klára noticed. Klára said 'you could call him Pavel.' Anna said nothing and kept saying 'on.'

phrase_or_pattern: Mixed Czech-English insults — 'he's such a dick, ale vlastně ne, he's just oblivious'
origin: With Tereza Bártová only. The mix is theirs, a language that belongs to no country, and it's the only way she can talk about Pavel without the words feeling like they belong to him. In English he's 'a dick.' In Czech he's 'on.' Between the two languages there's a space where she can say what she actually means.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' said at Pavel instead of about the fridge
origin: The word used to be Mum's, used to belong to the bumping and the anxiety and the small daily frustrations. Now Anna says it under her breath when Pavel's car pulls into the driveway. She doesn't know she's using her mother's language. She doesn't know the word is migrating, becoming hers, becoming the sound she makes when the world is too close.

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'fine' to Dad on FaceTime — one word, flat, a door closing
origin: Used to be 'alright, Munch?' 'Yeah, good, Dad, how are you?' Now it's 'alright, Munch?' 'Fine.' The word is a wall. The word is the distance between Croydon and Benešov made audible. She doesn't know she's doing it. She doesn't know he can hear the gap growing.

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'okay' to everything emotional — compliments, invitations, apologies
origin: From Lukáš Fiala. The word became a shield after it failed her in the corridor. 'Okay' closes doors. 'Okay' means the conversation is over. 'Okay' means she doesn't have to find the right words because there are no right words.

phrase_or_pattern: Dropping 'literally' into Czech sentences — 'já literally nemůžu'
origin: From the voice note. From English TikTok. From the part of her that still thinks in English when she's stressed. The word slides in without permission and marks her as what she is: a girl between two languages who can't keep them separate anymore.

phrase_or_pattern: Calling things 'cringe' in English instead of 'trapnej' — the Czech word exists but it doesn't hit the same
origin: From TikTok, from Elara Chung's old texts, from the English voice in her head that narrates her embarrassments. 'Cringe' is self-judgment in a language that doesn't belong to anyone in Benešov. It's private. 'Trapnej' is public.

phrase_or_pattern: Saying 'I'm fine' in English to herself at 3am, never in Czech
origin: From Dad. From the flat above Ladbrokes. From a version of herself that still lives in London and handles things in English because English is the language where she learned to lie.

phrase_or_pattern: Dropping Czech words into English sentences without noticing — 'he was being such a debil about it' and 'that's proper hrozný, like genuinely' and 'she's so namyšlená, you know?'
origin: Four years in Benešov. The Czech isn't just a second language anymore, it's eating the English from the inside, replacing words she used to know with words that feel faster, sharper, more accurate. 'Debil' hits harder than 'idiot.' 'Hrozný' covers more ground than 'terrible.' She doesn't notice she's doing it until Tereza points it out and then she notices it constantly, the mixed-up language of a mixed-up person.

phrase_or_pattern: True crime podcast phrases used as dark jokes — 'stay sexy and don't get murdered' from My Favorite Murder, said to Tereza when they walk home in the dark, both of them laughing at something that isn't actually funny
origin: The podcasts. The hours in the attic with English voices in her ears telling her about women who died. The phrase is armor — if you joke about murder, you can't be murdered. She knows this is irrational. She says it anyway.

phrase_or_pattern: Czech rap lyrics as internal monologue — Yzomandias lines running through her head during track, during fights, during the awful silence after she says something cruel
origin: The anger. The lyrics give her anger a container, a rhythm, a shape. When she can't articulate why she's furious, Viktor Sheen can, and the words aren't hers but they feel like hers, like borrowed weapons that fit her hands perfectly.

phrase_or_pattern: 'Proboha' — still using it, still feeling the phantom shame of being six and not knowing it was Czech
origin: Klára. The fridge. The bump. The word that was supposed to be English and wasn't. Every time she says it now she hears Markéta Dvořáková laughing and feels six again and stupid again and the word is hers now, truly hers, Czech and natural and automatic, but the ghost of that first shame still lives in it.

phrase_or_pattern: Short declarative sentences when she's uncomfortable — 'Fine.' 'Yeah.' 'Okay.' 'No.' — and longer run-on sentences when she's angry, the words piling up like cars in a crash
origin: The defensive architecture of a girl who learned early that words are weapons and silence is armor. The short sentences keep people out. The long sentences let the anger out. She doesn't have a middle register for normal conversation, just the two speeds — locked door and house fire.
